title-group.article-title: SARS-CoV-2 reinfections and subsequent risk of hospital-diagnosed post-acute sequelae in Denmark (2020–2022): a nationwide cohort study

contrib-group.aff: aSection of Health Data Science & AI, Department of Public Health, University of Copenhagen, Bartholinsgade 6Q, 1356, Copenhagen K, Denmark

bMRC Centre for Global Infectious Disease Analysis, Department of Infectious Disease Epidemiology, School of Public Health, Faculty of Medicine, Imperial College London, 90 Wood Lane, London, W12 0BZ, United Kingdom
cDepartment of Dermatology, Copenhagen University Hospital-Bispebjerg, Nielsine Nielsens Vej 9, 2400, Copenhagen, Denmark
dRockwool Foundation, Ny Kongensgade 6, 1472, Copenhagen, Denmark
fSection of Epidemiology, Department of Public Health, University of Copenhagen, Denmark
gDepartment of Virology and Microbiological Preparedness, Statens Serum Institut, Artillerivej 5, 2300, Copenhagen, Denmark

iInfectious Disease Epidemiology and Prevention, Statens Serum Institut, Artillerivej 5, 2300, Copenhagen, Denmark
jDepartment of Sequencing and Bioinformatics, Statens Serum Institut, Artillerivej 5, 2300, Copenhagen, Denmark

lEpidemiological Infectious Disease Preparedness, Statens Serum Institut, Artillerivej 5, 2300, Copenhagen, Denmark
nThe Kids Research Institute Australia, 15 Hospital Ave, Nedlands, WA, 6009, Australia

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), or long COVID, are a public health concern. While most recover from SARS-CoV-2 infections within weeks, some experience persistent symptoms. Here, we quantified the association between repeated SARS-CoV-2 infections and the risk of hospital-diagnosed PASC.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a nationwide register-based cohort study of all adults in Denmark (≥18 years) with at least one SARS-CoV-2 PCR or antigen test between April 1, 2020, and December 31, 2022. Participants were followed from first test until long COVID diagnosis (ICD-10: B948A), death, emigration, three SARS-CoV-2 infections, or end of study. Risk of long COVID diagnosis was estimated at three timepoints after study entry (180 days, 1 year, 2 years) and the outcomes were assessed during the 180 days after each timepoint. Cause-specific Cox models treated death as a competing risk, with number of infections and vaccination status as time-varying covariates. Absolute risks and differences were estimated using G-computation. Analyses were stratified by sex, income, and vaccination status. Secondary analyses assessed fatigue and headache (ICD-10), excluding individuals with prior diagnoses.

sec.title: Findings
sec.p: Of 4,418,544 individuals, 6942 (0.16%) were diagnosed with long COVID. The absolute risk of a diagnosis increased following reinfection (0.73% [95% CI 0.69–0.77] after one infection vs. 1.16% [1.05–1.30] after two infections at 180 days), but differences were small and decreased over time. Risks following reinfection were similar across sex and income strata. Absolute risk decreased with prior vaccinations. Secondary analyses showed no increased risk of fatigue or headache after primary infection. A small increase in fatigue risk was observed after reinfection at 1 year (RD 0.03% [0.01–0.05]), but not for headache.

sec.title: Interpretation
sec.p: Reinfection increases long COVID risk; however, the absolute increase after reinfection is smaller than that observed after a primary infection. Vaccination offers substantial protection against long COVID.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: 10.13039/501100001732Danish National Research Foundation (DNRF).

sec.title: Evidence before this study
sec.p: We searched PubMed for articles published between Dec 1, 2019, and Dec 31, 2025, without language restrictions, using the terms ((“COVID-19” OR “SARS-CoV-2”) AND (“long COVID” OR “post-acute sequelae” OR “post COVID condition” OR “PASC”) AND (“reinfection” OR “repeat infection” OR “multiple infections”)). The search returned 130 articles. Existing studies have identified demographic and clinical risk factors for post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 and have described the heterogeneous clinical manifestations of the condition. However, large, nationwide evidence on the impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfections on the risk of developing long COVID has so far been lacking. Most studies have been based on self-reported symptoms, small cohorts, or restricted follow-up, and few have examined potential effect modification by sociodemographic factors. In addition, the role of vaccination in modifying long COVID risk over longer time horizons remains underexplored.

sec.title: Added value of this study
sec.p: This nationwide study of more than 4 million adults in Denmark provides the most comprehensive assessment of long COVID risk following SARS-CoV-2 reinfection. Using linked registry data with near-complete follow-up, we estimated absolute and relative risks of long COVID diagnoses across infection episodes and examined potential effect modification by sex, socioeconomic status, and vaccination. By focusing on hospital-assigned ICD-10 codes, our analysis offers highly specific outcome definitions that complement prior studies based on self-reported symptoms. We further contribute novel evidence on symptom-specific outcomes (fatigue and headache) and on the protective association of vaccination against long COVID over longer follow-up periods.

sec.title: Implications of all the available evidence
sec.p: Reinfection with SARS-CoV-2 modestly increases the risk of long COVID, but the absolute excess risk is small compared with primary infection. Vaccination lowers the risk of long COVID, highlighting its role in reducing the long-term consequences of COVID-19. Sustained efforts to prevent reinfections remain important for limiting the population burden of post-acute sequelae.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Sequelae following viral infections have been observed across a range of pathogens, including Epstein–Barr, West Nile, and Chikungunya viruses.1, 2, 3 Following the initial SARS-CoV-2 infections at the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), also known as ‘long COVID’, were also identified.4, 5, 6, 7 Although the majority of individuals recover within weeks of infection onset, a subset develop persistent, multi-system health complications.6,8,9 Given the significant global burden of SARS-CoV-2 infections, concerns emerged regarding the long-term impacts of postinfection sequelae. PASC is recognised as a heterogeneous condition, encompassing a wide array of symptoms that persist beyond the acute phase of infection and are not attributable to other causes.6,10 Proposed mechanisms include immune dysregulation, autoimmunity, and endothelial dysfunction, though these represent only a subset of potential biological pathways leading from infection to post-infection sequelae.6,11, 12, 13, 14

Recent research has aimed to identify risk factors for PASC and quantify its burden.15, 16, 17, 18, 19 Furthermore, several studies have explored the effects of reinfections vaccination, clinical severity of infection, and SARS-CoV-2 variant.18,20, 21, 22 However, the relationship remains poorly measured and inadequately stratified, especially regarding sex, socioeconomic and vaccination status. Yet much of the available evidence relies on self-reported outcomes, small or highly selected cohorts, and relatively short follow-up. Large-scale cohort studies with standardised outcome measures and causal frameworks are needed to estimate absolute and relative risks more precisely and to clarify how preventing reinfections may reduce long-term sequelae. On a broader societal level, understanding the absolute risks of viral infections, such as that of SARS-CoV-2, on health outcomes is essential for mitigating the long-term health and socioeconomic consequences of epidemics and pandemics, as well as for evaluating the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of both pharmaceutical and non-pharmaceutical interventions.

In this nationwide cohort study of over 4 million Danish adults, we quantified the association between repeated SARS-CoV-2 infections and the risk of receiving an ICD-10 long COVID hospital diagnosis. Based on electronic health records from 2020 to 2022, we used a causal inference framework to estimate the absolute and relative risks of long COVID after multiple infection episodes, with results stratified by sex, socioeconomic status and vaccination status. To further elucidate symptom-specific associations with reinfection, we quantified the risks of being diagnosed with fatigue and headache, two commonly reported post-viral symptoms.6

sec.title: Study design and inclusion criteria
sec.p: We conducted a nationwide, register-based cohort study to examine the association between the number of SARS-CoV-2 infections, vaccination status, and the risk of long COVID among adults in Denmark. The cohort comprised all residents in Denmark aged 18 years and older who underwent at least one SARS-CoV-2 PCR or antigen test between April 1, 2020, and December 31, 2022; testing uptake in Denmark was high and broadly representative of the general population.23 Data were obtained from the Danish COVID-19 Surveillance Database, the Danish Vaccination Register, the Danish National Patient Registry (DNPR), and Statistics Denmark which cover all residents of Denmark. Pseudo-anonymised civil registration numbers (CPR numbers) enabled individual-level linkage across registries.

Individuals with a test prior to March 31, 2020, were excluded (n = 28,791) (Supplementary Figure S1) to ensure that all participants were at risk of receiving a long COVID diagnosis, which was only introduced on 1 April 2020, thereby avoiding immortal time bias. We restricted the cohort to tested individuals to ensure accurate ascertainment of SARS-CoV-2 infection status, as untested residents could not be reliably classified. Included study participants were followed from the date of their first SARS-CoV-2 PCR or antigen test performed at a public testing facility23 until the first occurrence of i) a long COVID outcome (see below), ii) emigration, iii) death, or iv) December 31, 2022.

sec.title: Outcomes and covariates
sec.p: PASC was primarily captured using the ICD-10 code ‘B948A’, corresponding to the clinical diagnosis of long COVID, which was introduced in Denmark on April 1, 2020, to identify complications persisting beyond the acute phase of SARS-CoV-2 infection and not attributable to other causes. These diagnoses reflect clinical assessments made in hospital settings, including outpatient specialty clinics, often following referral from general practitioners. In the Danish context, 94% percent of patients with the ICD-10 code have been shown to meet criteria of long COVID in subsequent audits of clinical record in 2020 and 2021.24 In Denmark, a long COVID diagnosis was assigned to patients with a positive SARS-CoV-2 test and persistent symptoms exceeding six weeks, including fatigue, dyspnoea, headache or neurocognitive deficits that could not be attributed to a different diagnosis.24 As secondary outcomes, we separately examined ICD-10 diagnoses for fatigue and headache (Supplementary Table S1),17 two common symptoms associated with post-viral syndromes,6 including SARS-CoV-2 infection. As with long COVID, these outcomes reflect clinically recorded diagnoses in secondary care settings and therefore capture only individuals with sufficiently persistent or severe symptoms to be documented in hospital records. Individuals with a prior diagnosis of fatigue or headache were excluded for this analysis (n = 64,967 for fatigue; n = 111,843 for headache). For both primary and secondary outcomes, we estimated absolute risks, absolute risk differences between exposure groups, and relative risk ratios.

Long COVID was analysed in relation to the number of prior infections. Infections were defined using a 90-day window, attributing all positive SARS-CoV-2 tests during that period to the same infection episode. A new episode was registered only if a positive test occurred more than 90 days after the previous test, consistent with existing definitions.25,26 Other covariates included age group (18–30 [18 ≤ age < 30], 30–45 [30 ≤ age < 45], 45–60 [45 ≤ age < 60], 60–75 [60 ≤ age < 75], 75+ [age ≥75]); sex (female, male); vaccination status (defined as cumulative count [0–3 vaccinations]); and the Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI).27 CCI values were tested using a 5-year look-back period from the start of follow-up. For socioeconomic status, we used equivalised disposable income (EQI). EQI is the total disposable income of a household after tax.28 The value is weighted according to number of adults and children in the household, based on the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development equivalence scale. In line with previous work in the Danish context,29 the mean income of up to 3 years before study inclusion was used to define an individual's income. Individuals were subsequently divided into income quartiles, with the first quartile (Q1) representing those in the lowest income bracket.

sec.title: Statistics
sec.p: Cause-specific Cox regression was used for the initial survival analysis using the riskRegression package,30 with the number of prior infections (0, 1, or 2) and vaccination status included as time-varying covariates. Death was treated as a competing risk, and individuals were censored at emigration, three SARS-CoV-2 infections or the end of follow up (31-Dec-2022). Age, sex, region of residence, Charlson comorbidity index, and income group were included per start of follow-up (Supplementary Figure S2). Log (-log) survival curves (Supplementary Figure S3) showed proportional and stable hazards, indicating robust estimation of the average effect over time and across major variants (Supplementary Table S2). As sensitivity analyses, cause-specific Cox models were stratified by major SARS-CoV-2 variant periods (Wildtype/Alpha and Delta/Omicron), and the main survival analyses were repeated using multiple imputation for missing values in region and income. Additionally, a negative-control analysis was conducted to evaluate the potential influence of ascertainment bias on the association between SARS-CoV-2 reinfections and long COVID, using hip fracture (ICD-10 S72) as an outcome.

To account for the time-varying covariates and confounding while targeting a population-level effect, model estimates were applied in a G-computation framework to calculate the marginal effect of infection on the risk of long COVID. G-computation allows for the estimation of counterfactual outcomes under different exposure scenarios, averaged across the covariate distribution.31 Individuals were evaluated at three prespecified follow-up times (hereafter referred to as landmarks): 180 days, 1 year, and 2 years after the start of follow-up. The 180-day landmark was chosen to ensure sufficient time for potential reinfections to occur, while also reflecting the fact that, by definition, no participant could have experienced a reinfection before 90 days following their initial infection. At each landmark, covariates were fixed, and outcomes were assessed over the following 180-day period. This approach aligns with definitions of long COVID, which typically require symptoms to persist for at least 90 days following infection.32

For each landmark, individuals with a prior long COVID diagnosis were excluded from the risk set, such that the reported absolute risks correspond to new diagnoses occurring within the 180-day interval. Because a subset of individuals received a long COVID diagnosis despite having no documented prior positive SARS-CoV-2 test, we conducted a sensitivity analysis restricting the cohort to individuals with at least one confirmed infection during follow-up to assess the influence of these cases. Due to the large sample size, 95% confidence intervals were estimated for G-computation using non-parametric bootstrap resampling (50 replicates for overall estimates, 100 for strata-specific estimates). This approach empirically estimates the sampling distribution without assuming normality.

Absolute risks, risk differences, and risk ratios were calculated per number of prior infections with pairwise comparisons across all exposure groups, representing marginal effects that reflect the covariate distribution of the study population. We also report the number needed to harm (NNH), calculated as the inverse of the absolute risk difference, representing the number of SARS-CoV-2 infections associated with one additional PASC outcome. All analyses were conducted using R version 4.3.2. Group differences in baseline characteristics were assessed using chi-square tests for categorical variables and the Wilcoxon rank-sum test for continuous variables, comparing individuals with vs. without the PASC outcome of interest.

sec.title: Ethics
sec.p: This study was conducted using data from national registers only. According to Danish law, ethics approval is not needed for this type of research. All data management and analyses were carried out on Statistics Denmark's secure research servers. The study only contains aggregated results and no personal data.

sec.title: Role of the funding source
sec.p: Funders had no role in study design, data collection, data analysis, data interpretation, or writing of the paper.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: When comparing individuals with only one infection to individuals with reinfection, we found that the mean absolute risk of long COVID was consistently higher among individuals with reinfection across all landmarks. Specifically, the cumulative risk for reinfection compared to primary infection was 1.58 (95% CI 1.44–1.77), 1.59 (95% CI 1.39–1.77), and 1.59 (95% CI 1.39–1.81) times higher for reinfections than for the primary infection at the 180 day, 1 year, and two year landmarks, respectively (Table 2). In a sensitivity analysis stratified by major variant periods, cause-specific hazard ratios for reinfection were broadly consistent across variants (2 infections vs. 1 infection: 2.08 [95% CI 1.23–3.52] for Wildtype/Alpha; 1.89 [95% CI 1.68–2.13] for Delta/Omicron; Supplementary Table S2), indicating that the observed associations were robust to differences in circulating variants. A negative-control analysis using hip fracture showed no increased risk among individuals with prior infections, supporting that the observed associations are unlikely to be explained by differential healthcare contact (Supplementary Table S3).

Although relative risks were elevated, absolute risk differences were small, particularly at later landmarks (2-year, see Table 2). At the 180-day landmark, the risk difference (RD) was 0.43% (95% CI 0.34–0.56, declining to 0.15% (95% CI 0.10–0.20%) at 1 year and 0.03% (95% CI 0.02–0.04%) at 2 years. These correspond to number needed to harm (NNH) estimates of 233 (95% CI 179–294) at 180 days, 667 (95% CI 510–1010) at 1 year, and 3257 (95% CI 2433–5102) at 2 years. Absolute risks of long COVID decreased with a higher number of prior vaccinations (Fig. 1), and this pattern remained consistent when analyses were restricted to the Delta/Omicron period and thereafter (Supplementary Figure S4).Fig. 1
Absolute risk of long COVID by number of prior SARS-CoV-2 vaccinations. Point estimates showing the absolute risk (%) of long COVID following one or two SARS-CoV-2 infections, with 95% confidence intervals indicated by error bars. Results are shown separately for a 180-day landmark and a 1-year landmark. Shapes differentiate between one or two infections. Risk of long COVID decreases with increasing number of vaccinations, with the lowest risk observed among individuals who received three vaccine doses. At the 180-day landmark, 5.2% of individuals had experienced at least one prior SARS-CoV-2 infection, increasing to 14.7% at the 1-year landmark among individuals still at risk. At the 180-day landmark, absolute risks were 1.18% (0.98–1.35%) and 1.98% (0.89–3.08%) after one and two infections with one dose, 0.53% (0.46–0.60%) and 0.84% (0.64–1.09%) with two doses, and 0.03% (0.02–0.04%) and 0.09% (0.06–0.13%) with three doses. At the 1-year landmark, absolute risks were 0.64% (0.52–0.77%) and 1.09% (0.37–1.72%) with one dose, 0.38% (0.35–0.42%) and 0.61% (0.51–0.77%) with two doses, and 0.05% (0.04–0.05%) and 0.14% (0.11–0.17%) with three doses.

The mean absolute risk of long COVID was lower following reinfection (2 vs. 1 infection) than following the primary infection (1 vs. 0 infections, see Table 2). At the 180-day landmark, the RD was 0.72% (95% CI 0.68–0.76%) after the primary infection (1 vs. 0 infections) compared to 0.43% (95% CI 0.34–0.56%) after reinfection (2 vs. 1 infections). A small subset of individuals diagnosed with long COVID had no documented prior positive test; however, these individuals were broadly similar to those with confirmed infections in terms of age, sex, and vaccination status, with modest differences by region and income (Supplementary Table S4). In a sensitivity analysis excluding individuals without any confirmed infections during follow-up, the risk differences and risk ratios at the 180-day landmark were broadly similar to the main analysis (Supplementary Figure S5). The risk difference for long COVID between individuals with reinfection vs. primary infection was 0.53% (95% CI 0.32–0.65%), corresponding to a risk ratio of 1.69 (95% CI 1.42–1.87). This suggests that the inclusion of individuals diagnosed with long COVID without confirmed infection did not meaningfully affect estimates of long COVID risk following reinfection. Additionally, a sensitivity analysis using multiple imputation for missing values in region and income showed similar results (Supplementary Table S6).

Differences in the effect of reinfection on long COVID by sex or socioeconomic status did not reach conventional levels of statistical significance. The effect was stronger among individuals with three vaccinations compared to those with zero or two vaccinations (Fig. 2), despite the mean absolute risk of long COVID being lower in this group (Fig. 1).Fig. 2
Association between reinfection and long COVID stratified by sex, income quartile, and vaccination status. Panels a–c show risk ratios (95% CIs) for reinfection (two infections) compared with a single infection at 180-day, 1-year, and 2-year landmarks, stratified by sex (a), income quartile (b), and number of COVID-19 vaccinations (c).

When PASC was defined as fatigue or headache (Table 1), we found no evidence that a primary SARS-CoV-2 infection increased the risk of either diagnosis. A marginally elevated risk of fatigue was observed following reinfection compared with primary infection at the 1-year landmark (RD 0.03%, 95% CI 0.01–0.05%, risk ratio [RR] 1.24, 95% CI 1.05–1.40) (Fig. 3a and b), although the absolute difference was small. Similar findings were observed at the 180-day landmark (Supplementary Figure S6). Associations between reinfection and the risk of headache diagnosis did not reach conventional levels of statistical significance.Fig. 3
Outcome measures for fatigue and headache. Absolute risk differences (a) and risk ratios (b) for fatigue (top row) and headache (bottom row) diagnoses by number of SARS-CoV-2 infections at the 1-year landmark. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection have emerged as a substantial and multifaceted consequence of COVID-19,4,11 yet the extent to which repeated infections contribute to this burden, and the magnitude of that contribution relative to primary infection, remained unclear. In this nationwide cohort of more than 4 million Danish adults between 2020 and 2022, reinfection with SARS-CoV-2 was associated with a higher absolute risk of receiving a long COVID diagnosis than primary infection across the whole follow-up period, although the excess risk from reinfection was modest and attenuated over time. The effect of reinfection on long COVID was similar across age groups, sex, and socioeconomic status. Vaccination reduced the risk of a long COVID diagnosis.

Consistent with previous studies, the risk of long COVID appears to increase with the number of infections, although the magnitude of this effect varies,18,33,34 likely reflecting differences in study populations, symptom assessment, and underlying biological mechanisms.6,11,32 Two estimates of long COVID prevalence after acute infection varied from a small minority of community cases to a substantial proportion of hospitalised patients,33,35 and a population-based surveillance study in the UK suggests that persistent symptoms are relatively uncommon but not rare.36 Our risk estimates of long COVID are also lower than symptom-based UK studies, which have previously reported prevalence of approximately 6%,9 reflecting the focus of our study on hospital-diagnosed cases, but are broadly comparable to UK primary care data, which report lower prevalence (around 0.5%).37 This variation reflects differences in study design, follow-up duration, healthcare access, and diagnostic thresholds, as well as the broad clinical spectrum of PASC, which can range from mild, transient complaints to severe, multi-system disability.32 Importantly, however, only a small fraction of individuals with post-acute sequelae ever received a formal long COVID diagnosis, underscoring that registry-based diagnoses may not capture the full burden of persistent symptoms. The true burden of PASC is therefore unknown and remains poorly characterised. Illustrating this broader burden, a recent population-based Danish survey found that, in a mostly vaccinated cohort, SARS-CoV-2 infection was associated with a small but significant increase in self-reported fatigue and a twofold increase in post-exertional malaise up to 18 months post-infection.5

Our findings indicate that some observed differences in long COVID diagnoses may reflect variations in health-seeking behaviour rather than underlying biology. While we observed no effect modification by sex, there was a trend toward stronger associations between reinfection and long COVID among individuals in higher income quartiles, which may reflect greater access to healthcare for evaluation of persistent symptoms.38 The link between reinfection and long COVID diagnosis was also more pronounced among individuals who had received three vaccine doses. This could reflect greater exposure to novel viral variants in breakthrough infections, or it may represent underlying differences in health-seeking behaviour, whereby individuals who are more likely to be vaccinated are also more likely to engage with healthcare services, undergo testing for reinfection, and report persistent symptoms, resulting in higher diagnostic ascertainment. Another possibility is that individuals with a higher burden of chronic illness remain more susceptible to both infection and PASC despite vaccination. Although our comorbidity index partially adjusts for this, residual confounding cannot be ruled out. Unmeasured confounders such as health behaviours (e.g., smoking, physical activity) or occupational exposures may influence both the likelihood of reinfection and long COVID diagnosis.

Separately, among individuals diagnosed with long COVID, a small subset had no documented positive SARS-CoV-2 test prior to diagnosis, representing misclassification of infection status. However, these individuals were broadly similar to those with at least one confirmed infection, with some differences by region and income. While this misclassification is a limitation, any resulting bias is likely minimal and unlikely to materially affect comparisons of long COVID risk between primary and reinfected cases. This consideration applies equally to fatigue and headache, where milder presentations managed in the community would not be captured in registry data. Importantly, however, our results indicate that the absolute risk of a long COVID diagnosis decreased as the number of prior vaccinations increased. These findings align with previous studies that have also demonstrated the protective effects of vaccination against long COVID.22,39

We found no link between primary infection and the risk of a fatigue or headache diagnosis, except a small increase in the likelihood of receiving a fatigue diagnosis. Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 are multifaceted and may reflect pathogenic mechanisms such as viral persistence, dysregulated immune responses, and endothelial inflammation.6,11 When symptoms are analysed individually as ICD10–coded outcomes rather than within a composite diagnosis, the signal for some may be too weak to detect, which underscores the importance of composite assessments of post-viral sequelae, especially when case definitions rely on hospital-based registration.

Our study has limitations. While data from the Danish national health registries enabled consistent ascertainment of long COVID throughout the study period, diagnoses were made in hospital settings and thus predominantly capture severe presentations. As a result, only a fraction of individuals experiencing post-acute sequelae are likely represented, underestimating the true risk of sequelae following SARS-CoV-2 infection. Individuals with greater comorbidity burden or healthcare-seeking behaviour may also have increased opportunity for diagnosis, introducing differential ascertainment bias. However, the absence of increased risk for unrelated outcomes, such as hip fracture, suggests that differential ascertainment is unlikely to fully explain our findings. Furthermore, the positive predictive value and sensitivity of long COVID diagnoses are lower among older individuals, potentially introducing misclassification bias in this population.6,24 Since ICD-10 codes are assigned by physicians, individuals with multiple registered infections might also be more likely to receive a diagnosis based on known previous exposure, which could have slightly inflated the associations with reinfection.

Additionally, our study was conducted in Denmark, which has universal healthcare, high testing rates, high vaccination coverage, and a relatively homogeneous population with strong public health infrastructure. Therefore, generalisability to settings with insurance-based healthcare, lower testing or vaccination rates, or more heterogeneous sociodemographic profiles may be limited. Testing practices also evolved over the study period. PCR testing was widely available early, while rapid antigen and home tests became more common from 2021 onward, with uptake varying by age, sex, vaccination status, and geography.23 From 2022, many infections were identified through home testing and are not captured in registries. However, because most participants began follow-up in 2020, the majority of infections occurred before home testing became widespread and given that the absolute risk of long COVID was low, any under-ascertainment of infections in 2022 is unlikely to meaningfully bias our risk estimates.

Overall, our findings indicate that SARS-CoV-2 reinfection is associated with an increased risk of receiving a long COVID diagnosis compared with primary infection, underscoring the importance of preventive measures, including vaccination, to reduce the total burden. Given the substantial functional impairment and distress associated with PASC, prioritizing strategies that limit reinfections should remain a central public health objective.

sec.title: Contributors
sec.p: M.P.K., A.K., D.A.D., M.M.B.S. & S.B. conceptualised the study. M.P.K. wrote the first full draft of the manuscript. M.P.K., N.S., & J.C.S. had access to and verified the raw data. M.P.K., A.K., & S.B. had final responsibility for the decision to submit for publication. All authors critically revised the manuscript for important intellectual content.

sec.title: Data sharing statement
sec.p: The Danish register data used for this research were obtained through Statistics Denmark, the central authority on Danish statistics. Due to privacy rules, we are not allowed to share these individual-level microdata. It is possible for individual researchers to gain access to the relevant microdata through Statistics Denmark's research services and reconstruct the data used in this study. All code relevant to reproducing the experiments is available online: https://github.com/mark-khurana/long_covid.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the manuscript preparation process
sec.p: During the preparation of this work the author(s) used ChatGPT (version 5) to correct grammar and refine code. After using this tool/service, the author(s) reviewed and edited the content as needed and take(s) full responsibility for the content of the published article.

sec.title: Declaration of interests
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This research was supported by the Danish National Research Foundation via a chair grant (DNRF160) which supports S.B., M.P.K. and N.S. S.B. and C.M. also acknowledges funding from the MRC Centre for Global Infectious Disease Analysis (reference MR/X020258/1), funded by the UK Medical Research Council (MRC). This UK funded award is carried out in the framework of the Global Health EDCTP3 Joint Undertaking. S.B. acknowledges support from The Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund For Strategic Innovation via the Schmidt Polymath Award (G-22-63345) which also supports C.M. S.B. acknowledges support from the 10.13039/501100009708Novo Nordisk Foundation via The Novo Nordisk Young Investigator Award (NNF20OC0059309). D.A.D. is supported by a Data Science–Emerging researcher award from 10.13039/501100009708Novo Nordisk Foundation (NNF23OC0084647). The contents of this publication are the sole responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the views of the funders. The authors express gratitude to Statens Serum Institut, Statistics Denmark and The Danish Health Data Authority for their efforts in gathering and granting access to the data.

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lanepe.2026.101601.
